FT_IC1.evidence: Supplement confirms EARS app data from ABCD Study participants (follow-up years 4, 5, and 6); 640 adolescents in final analytic sample from ABCD Study.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD EARS passive-sensing smartphone data; reports adjusted means of app-category time during school hours.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in JAMA (2026); no indication of duplicate status.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The only analyses reported are adjusted means of smartphone app-category time (minutes per school day) broken down by demographic groups (sex, race/ethnicity) and by SMAQ/MPIQ tertile. No numeric estimate pairs a phone/SM variable with an individual-trait-level outcome. Step 2 not needed. Other-side rule: The other side of all analyses is either (a) demographics (sex, race/ethnicity) — which are demographic-only and fail the other-side rule — or (b) SMAQ/MPIQ tertile groups — but SMAQ and MPIQ are themselves SM/phone variables (per binding clarification #1), not individual-trait outcomes. No individual-trait-level variable (mental health, cognition, behavior, etc.) appears on the other side of any analysis.

notes: PDF is supplement-only (eMethods + eReferences). Main paper (JAMA research letter) not available as full PDF but supplement provides sufficient detail to assess IC2. All analyses characterize smartphone app-category time during school hours by demographics and SMAQ/MPIQ tertile — no individual-trait outcome on the other side.

FT_IC1.evidence: "This study analyzed prospective data from 5356 US adolescents in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Year 2 (2018-2020, ages 11-12) and Year 4 (2020-2022, ages 13-14)."

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis; multivariable linear and Poisson regression of screen time and step count predicting BMI percentile and overweight/obesity risk.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Obesity (2026); no indication of duplicate status.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No estimate in any results location (Table 2, Figure 1, or supplementary tables S1-S2) uses a phone/SM-specific variable label (e.g., time on phone, time on SM, texting, social media). The only exposure is total recreational screen time from the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey. Step 2: Methods explicitly state the composite was derived by summing time across modalities including "watching television and videos, playing video games, texting, social media, video chat." This composite bundles TV and console/unspecified video games alongside phone/SM items without any separate per-modality breakout in results. Per binding clarification, a composite including TV and unspecified video games fails Step 2. IC2 = NOT_MET.

notes: BMI/overweight-obesity outcome is a qualifying individual-trait outcome. The paper fails IC2 solely because the screen time composite bundles TV and unspecified video games with phone/SM items and no per-modality breakout is provided anywhere in the paper.

FT_IC1.evidence: "We analyzed data from year 3 (2019-2021, 11-13 years old) to year 5 (2021-2023, 14-16 years old) of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study" with data from ABCD 6.0 release.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis; mixed-effects ordinal logistic regression and generalized linear models of media parenting practices predicting video game outcomes at years 4 and 5.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in World Journal of Pediatrics (2026); no indication of duplicate status.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Search of all results locations (Table 2, supplementary sex-stratified results, group-based trajectory models) finds no numeric estimate with a phone/SM-specific variable label. The IVs are media parenting practices (screen time modeling, mealtime screen use, bedroom screen use, use of screens to control behavior, monitoring, limiting) — not phone/SM variables. The DVs are video game outcomes: mature-rated video game frequency, problematic video game use (VGAQ), and weekend video game time. Step 2 not applicable since DVs are the screen variable side and no phone/SM variable is present on either side. Per binding clarification #2, gaming alone (VGAQ, generic video game time, mature-rated game frequency) does not qualify as a phone/SM variable unless explicitly mobile/phone-based or paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable. No such pairing exists anywhere in the paper. IC2 = NOT_MET.

notes: Study focuses entirely on media parenting practices predicting video game behaviors. Gaming variables (VGAQ, mature-rated game frequency, weekend video game time) are not phone/SM variables per the binding gaming carve-out. No phone or social media variable appears anywhere in the analysis.
